protocols.description: Kerberos is a symmetric-key network authentication protocol that uses a trusted third party (the Key Distribution Center, or KDC) to authenticate clients to services without transmitting passwords over the network. The flow has two phases: (1) Authentication Service (AS) Exchange: the client sends an AS-REQ to the KDC containing the client principal name and a pre-authentication data element (typically an encrypted timestamp using the client's long-term key derived from their password). The KDC validates the pre-authentication, generates a Ticket-Granting Ticket (TGT) encrypted with the KDC's secret key (krbtgt), and a session key encrypted with the client's long-term key. (2) Ticket-Granting Service (TGS) Exchange: when the client needs to access a service, it sends a TGS-REQ to the KDC containing the TGT and an authenticator encrypted with the TGT session key. The KDC issues a Service Ticket encrypted with the target service's long-term key. The client presents this Service Ticket to the service, which decrypts it, validates the authenticator, and grants access. Kerberos is the foundation of Active Directory authentication and is used extensively in Windows enterprise environments, Hadoop clusters, and other enterprise middleware.
protocols.security_considerations: The krbtgt account is the single most critical security asset in a Kerberos realm; compromise of the krbtgt password hash (via DCSync, NTDS.dit extraction, or other domain controller attacks) enables Golden Ticket attacks, allowing the attacker to forge TGTs for any user with arbitrary group memberships and expiration times. Rotate the krbtgt password twice in succession (to invalidate both current and previous keys) as part of incident response. Silver Ticket attacks exploit compromised service account password hashes to forge Service Tickets for specific services. Kerberoasting attacks target service accounts with SPNs (Service Principal Names) by requesting TGS tickets and offline-cracking their encrypted portions; mitigate by using Group Managed Service Accounts (gMSAs) with 240+ character auto-rotated passwords. AS-REP Roasting targets accounts without pre-authentication enabled (DONT_REQ_PREAUTH flag); audit and remediate these accounts. Enable AES-256 encryption (etype 18) and disable RC4/DES encryption types. Set TGT lifetime to 10 hours and renewal lifetime to 7 days (Active Directory defaults). Implement Kerberos constrained delegation (S4U) carefully; unconstrained delegation allows any service to impersonate any user to any other service, creating a massive privilege escalation vector. Monitor for anomalous TGT/TGS request patterns, encryption downgrade attempts, and tickets with abnormally long lifetimes.
protocols.rfc: RFC 4120 (Kerberos V5), RFC 4121 (Kerberos V5 GSS-API), RFC 6806 (Kerberos Principal Name Canonicalization)
protocols.sequence_diagram:
Client                  KDC (AS + TGS)           Service (e.g., File Server)
  |                          |                          |
  |  === AS Exchange ===     |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---AS-REQ---------------->|                          |
  |  (client principal,      |                          |
  |   pre-auth: encrypted    |                          |
  |   timestamp)             |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--AS-REP-----------------|                          |
  |  (TGT encrypted w/       |                          |
  |   krbtgt key,            |                          |
  |   session key encrypted  |                          |
  |   w/ client key)         |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |  [Client decrypts        |                          |
  |   session key]           |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |  === TGS Exchange ===    |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---TGS-REQ--------------->|                          |
  |  (TGT, authenticator     |                          |
  |   encrypted w/ session   |                          |
  |   key, target SPN)       |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--TGS-REP----------------|                          |
  |  (Service Ticket         |                          |
  |   encrypted w/ service   |                          |
  |   key, service session   |                          |
  |   key)                   |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |  === AP Exchange ===     |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---AP-REQ (Service Ticket + Authenticator)------>|
  |                          |                          |
  |<--AP-REP (mutual auth, optional)----------------|

protocols.description: LDAP Bind is the authentication operation in the Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (RFC 4511). It establishes the identity of the client to the directory server. There are three bind types: (1) Simple Bind sends the distinguished name (DN) and password in cleartext over the connection; this MUST only be used over TLS/LDAPS (port 636) or STARTTLS (port 389 upgraded to TLS) to protect credentials in transit. (2) Anonymous Bind sends an empty DN and password, granting the client whatever access the directory grants to unauthenticated users (which should be minimal or none in production). (3) SASL Bind (RFC 4422) supports pluggable authentication mechanisms including GSSAPI (Kerberos), EXTERNAL (client TLS certificate), DIGEST-MD5 (deprecated), and PLAIN (cleartext over secure channel). After a successful bind, the client's session is associated with the bound identity for authorization purposes. LDAP is the most widely deployed directory protocol in enterprise environments, underpinning Active Directory, OpenLDAP, 389 Directory Server, and virtually all enterprise identity stores. Modern deployments should prefer SASL/GSSAPI for Kerberos-integrated environments or SASL/EXTERNAL for certificate-based authentication.
protocols.security_considerations: Never use Simple Bind without TLS; the password is sent in cleartext and can be captured by any network observer. Even with TLS, Simple Bind transmits the password to the server, which must handle it securely. SASL/GSSAPI (Kerberos) is preferred because the password never leaves the client (the Kerberos ticket is used instead). Disable anonymous bind in production directory servers to prevent information disclosure (attackers can enumerate the directory structure, user accounts, and attributes). Disable DIGEST-MD5 SASL mechanism as it is deprecated (RFC 6331) and has known vulnerabilities. Enforce minimum TLS 1.2 for LDAPS and STARTTLS connections. Implement account lockout policies on the directory server to prevent brute-force password guessing via repeated bind attempts. Monitor for LDAP bind failures from unexpected source IPs, which may indicate credential stuffing attacks. Configure directory ACLs to restrict which attributes the bound identity can read (e.g., prevent non-admin users from reading password hashes or sensitive attributes like manager, employeeNumber). LDAP injection attacks can occur if user input is incorporated into LDAP search filters without proper escaping; sanitize all user input used in LDAP operations. Implement connection rate limiting and maximum bind attempts per IP per time window.
protocols.sequence_diagram:
Client                  LDAP Directory Server
  |                          |
  |  === STARTTLS (optional) |
  |                          |
  |---Extended Operation---->|
  |  (OID: 1.3.6.1.4.1.     |
  |   1466.20037 = StartTLS) |
  |                          |
  |<--Result: Success--------|
  |                          |
  |  [TLS Handshake]         |
  |<=========================|
  |                          |
  |  === Simple Bind ===     |
  |                          |
  |---Bind Request---------->|
  |  (version: 3,            |
  |   name: cn=user,dc=...,  |
  |   authentication: simple,|
  |   password: ******)      |
  |                          |
  |<--Bind Response----------|  
  |  (resultCode: success    |
  |   OR invalidCredentials) |
  |                          |
  |  === SASL/GSSAPI Bind == |
  |                          |
  |---Bind Request---------->|
  |  (authentication: SASL,  |
  |   mechanism: GSSAPI,     |
  |   credentials: Kerberos  |
  |   token)                 |
  |                          |
  |<--Bind Response----------|

protocols.description: LDAP Search is the primary read operation in LDAP, enabling clients to query directory entries based on a base DN, search scope, filter expression, and requested attributes. The search operation takes several parameters: (1) baseDN specifies the starting point in the directory tree (e.g., 'ou=users,dc=example,dc=com'). (2) scope determines the breadth of the search: baseObject (only the entry at baseDN), singleLevel (immediate children only), or wholeSubtree (baseDN and all descendants). (3) filter is an expression using LDAP filter syntax (RFC 4515) such as '(&(objectClass=person)(department=Engineering)(!(disabled=TRUE)))'. (4) attributes lists which attributes to return (e.g., cn, mail, memberOf). (5) sizeLimit and timeLimit control resource consumption. The server returns zero or more SearchResultEntry messages followed by a SearchResultDone message. LDAP Search is the backbone of directory-based authorization checks, group membership lookups, user attribute retrieval for SAML/OIDC claim generation, and organizational structure navigation. Efficient LDAP queries with proper indexing are critical for directory performance.
protocols.security_considerations: LDAP injection is the primary application-layer threat: if user input is incorporated into LDAP filter expressions without proper escaping, attackers can modify the filter logic to bypass authentication checks (e.g., changing '(uid=INPUT)' to '(uid=*)(|' to match all users) or enumerate sensitive data. Escape special characters (*, (, ), \, NUL) per RFC 4515 Section 3. Implement Access Control Instructions (ACIs) or ACLs to restrict which entries and attributes each bound identity can search. Sensitive attributes (userPassword, unicodePwd, supplementalCredentials) should never be readable via LDAP search, even by authenticated users. Set server-side sizeLimit (e.g., 1000 entries) and timeLimit (e.g., 30 seconds) to prevent denial-of-service via expensive searches that traverse the entire directory tree. Monitor for enumeration patterns: repeated searches with incrementing filters (uid=a*, uid=b*) may indicate reconnaissance. Use the ManageDsaIT control cautiously as it can expose referral entries. Index frequently searched attributes to prevent full directory scans. In Active Directory, be aware that LDAP searches can reveal sensitive information through member/memberOf attributes, msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo (delegation configuration), and servicePrincipalName (Kerberoasting targets) attributes.
protocols.rfc: RFC 4511 (LDAP Protocol), RFC 4515 (LDAP String Representation of Search Filters)
protocols.sequence_diagram:
Client                  LDAP Directory Server
  |                          |
  |  [Already authenticated  |
  |   via Bind]              |
  |                          |
  |---Search Request-------->|
  |  (baseDN: ou=users,      |
  |   dc=example,dc=com,     |
  |   scope: wholeSubtree,   |
  |   filter: (&             |
  |     (objectClass=person) |
  |     (department=Eng)),   |
  |   attributes: [cn, mail, |
  |     memberOf],           |
  |   sizeLimit: 100,        |
  |   timeLimit: 30)         |
  |                          |
  |<--SearchResultEntry------|  
  |  (dn: cn=Alice...,       |
  |   cn: Alice Smith,       |
  |   mail: alice@...,       |
  |   memberOf: [...])       |
  |                          |
  |<--SearchResultEntry------|  
  |  (dn: cn=Bob...,         |
  |   cn: Bob Jones,         |
  |   mail: bob@...,         |
  |   memberOf: [...])       |
  |                          |
  |<--SearchResultDone-------|  
  |  (resultCode: success,   |
  |   matchedDN, diagnostics)|

protocols.description: RADIUS (Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service) is a client-server protocol for centralized authentication, authorization, and accounting (AAA) for network access. Originally designed for dial-up connections, RADIUS is now ubiquitous in enterprise networks for authenticating VPN connections, Wi-Fi access (802.1X), wired network access (802.1X), and network device administration. The RADIUS flow begins when a Network Access Server (NAS) — such as a VPN concentrator, wireless access point, or network switch — receives a connection attempt from a user. The NAS constructs an Access-Request packet containing the user's credentials (username and either a password encrypted with the shared secret, or EAP data for 802.1X), and sends it to the RADIUS server over UDP (port 1812 for authentication, port 1813 for accounting). The RADIUS server validates the credentials against its user store (often backed by LDAP or Active Directory), evaluates authorization policies, and returns either an Access-Accept (with authorization attributes such as VLAN assignment, ACLs, session timeout), an Access-Reject, or an Access-Challenge (for multi-step authentication flows like EAP-TLS, EAP-PEAP, or EAP-TTLS). The NAS enforces the returned authorization attributes on the network session.
protocols.security_considerations: The RADIUS shared secret is used to encrypt the User-Password attribute (using MD5-based XOR) and to authenticate/integrity-protect packets via the Response Authenticator and Message-Authenticator attributes. MD5 is cryptographically weak; use strong shared secrets (at least 16 random characters) and unique secrets per NAS. The shared secret is never transmitted but must be configured identically on the NAS and RADIUS server, creating operational overhead. RADIUS over UDP is vulnerable to packet injection and replay attacks if the Message-Authenticator attribute (HMAC-MD5) is not required. RadSec (RADIUS over TLS, RFC 6614) addresses these transport-level weaknesses by running RADIUS inside a TLS tunnel with certificate-based mutual authentication, eliminating the shared secret model. For wireless 802.1X, prefer EAP-TLS (client certificates) over EAP-PEAP/MSCHAPv2, as MSCHAPv2 is vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks if the outer TLS tunnel is compromised (via rogue AP or TLS interception). Never use PAP over unencrypted RADIUS. Implement RADIUS server redundancy (primary/secondary) and monitor for authentication failures that may indicate credential attacks. Separate RADIUS servers for network administration from those used for user access to limit the blast radius of compromise.
protocols.rfc: RFC 2865 (RADIUS), RFC 3579 (RADIUS EAP), RFC 6614 (RadSec/RADIUS over TLS)
protocols.sequence_diagram:
User/Supplicant         NAS (Access Point/VPN)   RADIUS Server        User Store (LDAP/AD)
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |---Connect/Associate----->|                          |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |<--Identity Request-------|                          |                     |
  |---Username/Credentials-->|                          |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |---Access-Request-------->|                     |
  |                          |  (User-Name,             |                     |
  |                          |   User-Password or       |                     |
  |                          |   EAP-Message,           |                     |
  |                          |   NAS-IP, NAS-Port,      |                     |
  |                          |   Message-Authenticator) |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |                          |---Validate--------->|
  |                          |                          |<--Result------------|  
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |                          |<--Access-Accept----------|                     |
  |                          |  (Filter-Id, VLAN,       |                     |
  |                          |   Session-Timeout,       |                     |
  |                          |   Tunnel-Type)           |                     |
  |                          |                          |                     |
  |<--Network Access---------|                          |                     |
  |  (authorized per RADIUS  |                          |                     |
  |   attributes)            |                          |                     |

protocols.description: WS-Federation (Web Services Federation) Passive Profile is a browser-based SSO protocol that was developed by Microsoft and IBM as part of the WS-* web services security specifications. It defines how identity information (security tokens) is requested and exchanged between an Identity Provider (IP/STS — Security Token Service) and a Relying Party (RP) using browser redirects and form posts. In the passive flow, a user accesses a protected resource at the RP, which redirects the browser to the IP/STS with a wsignin1.0 action, the RP's realm (wtrealm), and a reply URL (wreply). The STS authenticates the user and returns a WS-Federation response containing a RequestSecurityTokenResponse (RSTR) with a SAML 1.1 or SAML 2.0 security token via HTTP POST to the RP's reply URL. The RP validates the token and establishes a session. WS-Federation also defines logout (wsignout1.0, wsignoutcleanup1.0) and token renewal flows. While largely superseded by OIDC for new deployments, WS-Federation remains widely deployed in Microsoft-centric enterprise environments, particularly with Active Directory Federation Services (ADFS), Azure AD (which supports it alongside OIDC and SAML), and older SharePoint and .NET applications.
protocols.security_considerations: WS-Federation tokens (typically SAML assertions wrapped in RSTR) must be validated with the same rigor as standalone SAML assertions: verify the XML signature, check NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter conditions, validate the audience (wtrealm must match the RP's expected realm), and implement replay detection. WS-Federation does not define PKCE or nonce-equivalent mechanisms natively, making it more susceptible to CSRF and replay attacks compared to OIDC. Implement the wctx parameter (context/state) as CSRF protection and validate it upon return. The wreply parameter (equivalent to redirect_uri) must be strictly validated against registered reply URLs to prevent open redirect attacks. XML-based token processing is vulnerable to the same XML-specific attacks as SAML: XML signature wrapping, XXE, XML bombs, and canonicalization attacks. Disable DTD processing in XML parsers. For new deployments, migrate to OIDC which provides simpler, JSON-based tokens with built-in PKCE and nonce protection. When maintaining existing WS-Federation deployments, keep ADFS/STS software up to date, use strong token signing certificates (RSA 2048+ or ECDSA), and rotate signing certificates according to your certificate management policy. Monitor the token signing certificate's expiration and have a documented rotation procedure.
protocols.sequence_diagram:
User                    Relying Party            IP/STS (ADFS)
  |                          |                          |
  |---Access Resource------->|                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |  [No session/token]      |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--Redirect to STS--------|                          |
  |  (wa=wsignin1.0,         |                          |
  |   wtrealm=RP realm,      |                          |
  |   wreply=RP reply URL,   |                          |
  |   wctx=state)            |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---wsignin1.0 request-----|---(via browser)--------->|
  |                          |                          |
  |                          |    User authenticates    |
  |                          |    (Kerberos/Forms/MFA)  |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--POST to wreply---------|--------------------------|  
  |  (wresult= RSTR          |                          |
  |   containing SAML token, |                          |
  |   wctx=state)            |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |--->POST RSTR to RP------>|                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |  [RP validates token     |                          |
  |   signature, conditions, |                          |
  |   audience, creates      |                          |
  |   session cookie]        |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--Protected Resource-----|                          |

protocols.description: DEPRECATED. NTLM (NT LAN Manager) is a legacy challenge-response authentication protocol used in Windows environments. The flow is a three-message handshake: (1) NEGOTIATE: the client sends a negotiate message indicating supported NTLM features and flags. (2) CHALLENGE: the server responds with a server challenge (8-byte random nonce) and supported feature flags. (3) AUTHENTICATE: the client computes an NT response by hashing the user's password (using MD4 to create the NT hash, then using the NT hash with HMAC-MD5 and the server challenge to create the NTLMv2 response) and sends it along with the username, domain, and session key material. The server validates the response either locally or by forwarding it to a domain controller via Netlogon. NTLM is deprecated by Microsoft in favor of Kerberos for domain authentication and modern protocols (OIDC, SAML) for web authentication. However, NTLM remains widely deployed as a fallback when Kerberos fails (e.g., when accessing resources by IP address rather than hostname, or when the client cannot reach a domain controller for TGT acquisition). Windows Server 2025 introduces options to disable NTLM entirely.
protocols.security_considerations: DO NOT USE for new implementations. NTLM has fundamental cryptographic weaknesses and is targeted by numerous well-known attacks. Pass-the-Hash (PtH): the NT hash is functionally equivalent to the password for authentication purposes; an attacker who obtains the hash (from SAM database, lsass.exe memory, or NTDS.dit) can authenticate without knowing the plaintext password. NTLM Relay: an attacker positioned between client and server can relay NTLM authentication messages to a different target server, gaining unauthorized access (tools: ntlmrelayx, Responder). EPA (Extended Protection for Authentication) and Channel Binding (RFC 5929) mitigate relay attacks but must be explicitly enabled on each service. NTLMv1 uses DES-based encryption that is trivially crackable; disable NTLMv1 entirely and require NTLMv2 at minimum. Even NTLMv2 hashes can be cracked offline with modern GPU hardware. LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning enables attackers to intercept NTLM authentication on local networks (Responder tool); disable these legacy name resolution protocols. Migration strategy: (1) Audit NTLM usage with Windows event ID 4624 (logon type) and NTLM auditing policies. (2) Remediate applications and services that require NTLM. (3) Enable Kerberos for all domain-joined resources. (4) Block NTLM progressively using Group Policy (Network Security: Restrict NTLM).
protocols.rfc: MS-NLMP (Microsoft NTLM Protocol Specification) — no IETF RFC; proprietary protocol
protocols.sequence_diagram:
Client                  Server/NAS               Domain Controller
  |                          |                          |
  |---NEGOTIATE Message----->|                          |
  |  (NTLM flags,            |                          |
  |   supported features)    |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |<--CHALLENGE Message------|                          |
  |  (server challenge:      |                          |
  |   8-byte nonce,          |                          |
  |   target info,           |                          |
  |   NTLM flags)            |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |  [Client computes NT     |                          |
  |   response using:        |                          |
  |   MD4(password) = NThash |                          |
  |   HMAC-MD5(NThash,       |                          |
  |   challenge+blob) =      |                          |
  |   NTLMv2 response]       |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |---AUTHENTICATE Message-->|                          |
  |  (NTLMv2 response,       |                          |
  |   username, domain,      |                          |
  |   workstation)           |                          |
  |                          |                          |
  |                          |---Netlogon Validate----->|
  |                          |  (NTLM pass-through)     |
  |                          |                          |
  |                          |<--Validation Result------|  
  |                          |                          |
  |<--Auth Success/Failure---|                          |
